Quantile Regression Analysis

Demographic and Socioeconomic Predictors of Algebra 1 End-of-Course Achievement in Florida School Districts

Advisor: Dr. Cohen

Authors

Wendy Matende

Madison Edwards

Jegg Ononiewu

Published

July 6, 2026

Introduction

Algebra 1 is widely recognized as a foundational gateway course in secondary mathematics, shaping students’ access to advanced coursework and long-term academic opportunities. Success in Algebra 1 is strongly associated with readiness for higher-level mathematics, college preparation, and career pathways in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) fields. In Florida, the stakes are particularly high because students must pass the Algebra 1 End-of-Course (EOC) assessment—or earn an approved comparative score—to satisfy the graduation requirement for a standard high school diploma. In addition, the EOC assessment contributes 30% of a student’s final course grade and serves an important role in school accountability and instructional decision-making. As noted in Florida Department of Education guidance, the assessment influences “course completion, graduation, school accountability, and decisions about remediation and student support.”

Florida’s 67 school districts vary considerably in their demographic and socioeconomic composition, including racial and ethnic distributions, poverty levels, unemployment rates, household income, and educational attainment. These contextual characteristics have long been associated with differences in educational outcomes, particularly mathematics achievement. Students living in economically disadvantaged communities often experience structural barriers that may limit academic success, whereas districts with greater socioeconomic resources may provide conditions that better support student learning and achievement. Understanding how these community characteristics relate to student performance is therefore essential for developing equitable educational policies and interventions.

Previous research suggests that demographic and socioeconomic influences on achievement are complex and may not operate uniformly across all levels of student performance. Tian (2006) found that family background and socioeconomic characteristics affect lower-achieving and higher-achieving students differently, suggesting that contextual influences vary across the achievement distribution. Similarly, Haile and Nguyen (2008) reported that ethnicity-related effects differ across performance levels, indicating that analyses focused solely on average outcomes may obscure important variation. Reeves and Lowe (2009) further cautioned that mean-based statistical models can encourage “one-size-fits-all” interpretations by masking meaningful differences among lower-, middle-, and higher-performing groups. Despite these findings, most studies examining predictors of mathematics achievement continue to rely on ordinary least squares (OLS) regression, which estimates average relationships and assumes that predictor effects remain constant across the entire outcome distribution. In contrast, quantile regression estimates relationships at multiple points of the distribution, allowing researchers to determine whether predictors exert stronger, weaker, or qualitatively different effects among lower-, middle-, and higher-performing districts.

Although quantile regression has been applied in a variety of educational settings, relatively little research has examined district-level Algebra 1 EOC achievement using this approach, particularly within Florida. Existing Florida studies, including Holley (2017), have explored factors associated with Algebra 1 EOC performance but have primarily relied on traditional mean-based statistical methods. Consequently, an important gap remains in understanding whether demographic and socioeconomic characteristics influence Algebra 1 achievement differently across districts with varying levels of academic performance. As noted in prior work, statewide averages “cannot show whether the same relationships appear in lower, middle-, and higher-performing districts,” highlighting the need for analyses that account for differences across the achievement distribution.

Addressing this gap, the present study examines how district-level demographic characteristics (White, Black, and Hispanic population estimates) and socioeconomic characteristics (poverty rate, unemployment rate, median household income, and bachelor’s degree attainment) are associated with Algebra 1 EOC achievement across Florida school districts and whether these relationships differ across lower-, middle-, and higher-achievement quantiles. Quantile regression is employed to estimate the effects of these predictors at multiple points of the achievement distribution, providing a more comprehensive understanding of how contextual factors relate to mathematics performance than traditional mean-based approaches.

Specifically, this study addresses the following research questions:

  1. How are district-level demographic characteristics associated with Algebra 1 EOC achievement?

  2. How are district-level socioeconomic characteristics associated with Algebra 1 EOC achievement?

  3. Do the direction, magnitude, and statistical significance of these predictors vary across the achievement distribution when examined using quantile regression?

  4. Which predictors demonstrate the strongest associations at specific quantiles, and what implications do these patterns hold for equity-focused district planning and decision-making?

The findings of this study have practical implications for educational leaders, policymakers, and researchers. Identifying whether particular demographic or socioeconomic characteristics exert stronger influences among lower-performing or higher-performing districts may support more targeted resource allocation, equity-focused planning, and evidence-based decision-making. For educators, the results provide insight into how community context may shape mathematics achievement and can inform efforts to reduce disparities in student outcomes. For researchers, this study extends the application of quantile regression within K–12 educational research while addressing a documented gap in the Florida Algebra 1 EOC literature. By examining achievement through a distribution-sensitive lens, this study contributes to a more nuanced understanding of how demographic and socioeconomic conditions are associated with mathematics performance and offers evidence that may inform policies and interventions designed to promote educational success across Florida’s diverse school districts.

Methods

Quantile Regression (QR) was used as the primary analytical method to examine how demographic and socioeconomic characteristics relate to district-level Algebra 1 End-of-Course (EOC) achievement across the performance distribution. QR, introduced by Koenker and Bassett (1978), extends traditional least squares estimation by allowing researchers to model conditional quantiles rather than only the conditional mean. Reeves and Lowe (2009) emphasize that “OLS regression coefficients may mislead” because they describe average relationships, whereas QR “allows variation in the conditional distribution to be examined” (p. 178). Costanzo and Desimoni (2017) similarly argue that mean-based models “represent only a part of the information concerning the complex and nuanced nature” of educational outcomes (p. 132). These methodological insights support the use of QR in contexts where achievement gaps and socioeconomic influences may differ across performance levels.

For each quantile, \(\tau\), the conditional quantile function was specified as:

\[ \begin{aligned} Q_\tau(Y|X) &= \beta_0(\tau) + \beta_1(\tau)\text{pct\_white} + \beta_2(\tau)\text{pct\_black} + \beta_3(\tau)\text{pct\_hispanic} \\ &\quad + \beta_4(\tau)\text{median\_income} + \beta_5(\tau)\text{poverty\_rate} \\ &\quad + \beta_6(\tau)\text{unemployment\_rate} + \beta_7(\tau)\text{bachelors\_rate}. \end{aligned} \]

where \(Y\) represents the district-level mean Algebra 1 EOC scale score, \(X\) represents the vector of demographic and socioeconomic predictors, \(\beta(\tau)\) denotes the quantile-specific regression coefficients, and \(\tau\) denotes the conditional quantile being estimated. This formulation allows the effects of demographic and socioeconomic characteristics to vary across different points of the achievement distribution, consistent with the QR framework described by Koenker and Ng (2005) and Davino et al. (2013).

This study employed a quantitative, cross-sectional research design using two publicly available datasets. The first dataset was the Spring 2024 Florida Algebra 1 End-of-Course assessment data obtained from the Florida Department of Education. The dataset included district-level measures such as the number of students tested (tested_count), mean scale score, percentages of students achieving Levels 1 through 5, and the percentage of students scoring at Level 3 or above. The second dataset consisted of the 2023 American Community Survey (ACS) 5-Year Estimates from the U.S. Census Bureau, which provided demographic and socioeconomic characteristics including the percentage of White, Black, and Hispanic residents (pct_white, pct_black, and pct_hispanic), median household income (median_income), poverty rate (poverty_rate), unemployment rate (unemployment_rate), and the percentage of adults with a bachelor’s degree or higher (bachelors_rate).

Both datasets were cleaned and standardized prior to analysis. Districts with missing or suppressed EOC achievement data were excluded from the analysis. ACS variables originally reported as population counts were converted to percentages or rates where appropriate to facilitate comparisons across districts. Because most Florida public school districts correspond to county boundaries, county-level ACS estimates were merged with district-level EOC data using county identifiers. After merging the datasets and removing incomplete observations, the final analytic sample consisted of 66 districts. All data preparation and statistical analyses were conducted using R.

The dependent variable was the district-level mean Algebra 1 EOC scale score. Independent variables included district demographic composition and socioeconomic characteristics obtained from the ACS dataset. These variables were selected based on prior research identifying demographic composition and socioeconomic conditions as important predictors of academic achievement (Haile and Nguyen (2008); Costanzo and Desimoni (2017)). Percentages of students achieving Levels 1 through 5 and the percentage scoring at Level 3 or above were retained for descriptive purposes but were not included as explanatory variables because they represent alternative measures of the same assessment outcome.

Quantile regression models were estimated at the 10th, 25th, 50th, 75th, and 90th quantiles to examine whether the relationships between demographic and socioeconomic characteristics and Algebra 1 achievement differed across lower-, middle-, and higher-performing districts. For comparison, an Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression model using the same predictors was also estimated, consistent with the recommendations of Reeves and Lowe (2009). Statistical inference for both OLS and quantile regression models was evaluated using a significance level of \(\alpha = .05\).

Because comparable district-level measures of school characteristics, including attendance rates, student–teacher ratios, English Language Learner enrollment, and students with disabilities, were not publicly available, these variables were not included in the analysis. County-level ACS estimates were used as proxies for district demographic and socioeconomic characteristics when merged with district-level achievement data.

Below is the complete data processing program used to ingest, format, and merge the target education records and census fields.

▶ Data Cleaning and Preparation Code
View R Code 
library(tidyverse)

View R Code 
library(tidycensus)
library(janitor)

Attaching package: 'janitor'

The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    chisq.test, fisher.test
View R Code 
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)

eoc_raw <- read_excel("34Spring24Alg1SRD (1).xls", skip = 4)

eoc_clean <- eoc_raw %>%
mutate(`District Number` = as.numeric(`District Number`)) %>%
filter(`District Number` >= 1 & `District Number` <= 67)

eoc_clean2 <- eoc_clean %>%
filter(Grade == "All Grades")

library(tidyverse)
library(tidycensus)
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

readRenviron("~/.Renviron")

acs_raw <- get_acs(
geography = "county",
state = "FL",
survey = "acs5",
year = 2023,
variables = c(
total_pop = "B03002_001",

white = "B03002_003",
black = "B03002_004",
hispanic = "B03002_012",

poverty_total = "B17001_001",
poverty = "B17001_002",

labor_force = "B23025_003",
unemployed = "B23025_005",

pop25plus = "B15003_001",
bachelors = "B15003_022",

median_income = "B19013_001"
)
)
Getting data from the 2019-2023 5-year ACS
View R Code 
acs_wide <- acs_raw %>%
select(GEOID, NAME, variable, estimate) %>%
pivot_wider(
names_from = variable,
values_from = estimate
)

acs_wide <- acs_wide %>%
mutate(
pct_white = 100 * white / total_pop,
pct_black = 100 * black / total_pop,
pct_hispanic = 100 * hispanic / total_pop,
poverty_rate = 100 * poverty / poverty_total,
unemployment_rate = 100 * unemployed / labor_force,
bachelors_rate = 100 * bachelors / pop25plus
)

acs_final <- acs_wide %>%
select(
GEOID,
NAME,
pct_white,
pct_black,
pct_hispanic,
poverty_rate,
unemployment_rate,
median_income,
bachelors_rate
)

acs_final <- acs_final %>%
mutate(
District_Name = toupper(gsub(" County, Florida", "", NAME))
)

eoc_clean2 <- eoc_clean2 %>%
mutate(
District_Name = toupper(`District Name`)
)


final_data2 <- eoc_clean2 %>%
left_join(
acs_final,
by = "District_Name"
)

final_data2 <- final_data2[-34, ]

Results and Discussion

This section presents the findings of the study in the order of the analyses conducted. Each subsection briefly describes the statistical results, provides interpretation, situates findings within existing literature, and explicitly connects outcomes to the research questions.

View R Code 
final_data2 <- final_data2 %>%
rename(
Mean_Score = `Mean Scale Score`,
Level3Plus = `Percentage in \nLevel 3 or Above`
)
View R Code 
final_data2$Mean_Score <- as.numeric(trimws(final_data2$Mean_Score))

Distribution of Algebra 1 Mean Scale Scores (Figure 1)

View R Code 
ggplot(final_data2, aes(x = Mean_Score)) +
geom_histogram(bins = 10, fill = "steelblue") +
labs(
title = "Distribution of Algebra 1 Mean Scale Scores",
x = "Mean Scale Score",
y = "Frequency"
) +
theme_minimal()

Distribution of Algebra 1 Mean Scale Scores (Figure 1)

Figure 1 shows that district-level Algebra 1 mean scale scores are tightly clustered between approximately 390 and 405, with limited variation across Florida school districts.

This narrow distribution suggests that most districts perform close to the state average, reflecting standardized assessment conditions and statewide accountability structures that reduce variability in outcomes. However, even within a compressed range, small differences in performance may reflect meaningful underlying socioeconomic inequalities.

This pattern is consistent with Reardon et al. (2019), who notes that district-level achievement distributions often appear compressed due to standardized systems, yet still mask persistent socioeconomic gradients. Similarly, Sirin (2005) emphasizes that socioeconomic status continues to explain variation even when overall dispersion is limited.

Answer to Research Question 1: The figure establishes the baseline distribution of achievement and confirms that district performance varies within a narrow but meaningful range.

Poverty and Algebra 1 Achievement (Figure 2)

View R Code 
ggplot(final_data2, aes(x = poverty_rate, y = Mean_Score)) +
geom_point(size = 3) +
geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
labs(
title = "Poverty and Algebra 1 Achievement",
x = "Poverty Rate",
y = "Mean Scale Score"
) +
theme_minimal()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Figure 2 shows a negative relationship between poverty and Algebra 1 achievement, with higher-poverty districts generally exhibiting lower mean scores.

This suggests that socioeconomic disadvantage is associated with lower academic performance. However, poverty loses statistical significance in the multivariate model, indicating that its effect overlaps with other socioeconomic variables such as income and educational attainment.

This finding aligns with Sirin (2005) and Reardon et al. (2019), who argue that poverty reflects broader structural disadvantage rather than acting independently.

Answer to Research Question 2: Poverty is negatively associated with Algebra 1 achievement, but its independent effect is reduced when other socioeconomic factors are included.

Median Household Income (Figure 3)

View R Code 
ggplot(final_data2, aes(x = median_income, y = Mean_Score)) +
geom_point(size = 3) +
geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
labs(
title = "Median Income and Algebra 1 Achievement",
x = "Median Household Income",
y = "Mean Scale Score"
) +
theme_minimal()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Figure 3 shows a clear positive association between median household income and Algebra 1 achievement.

Districts with higher income levels tend to perform better academically, although this relationship becomes statistically insignificant in the multivariate model due to strong correlation with educational attainment.

This is consistent with Reardon et al. (2019) and Duncan and Murnane (2016), who show that income strongly predicts achievement but often operates through correlated socioeconomic pathways.

Answer to Research Question 2: Median income is positively associated with achievement, but its unique effect is reduced due to multicollinearity with other SES variables.

Bachelor’s Degree Attainment (Figure 4)

View R Code 
ggplot(final_data2, aes(x = bachelors_rate, y = Mean_Score)) +
geom_point(size = 3) +
geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
labs(
title = "Bachelor’s Degree Attainment and Achievement",
x = "Bachelor’s Degree Rate",
y = "Mean Scale Score"
) +
theme_minimal()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Figure 4 shows a strong positive relationship between bachelor’s degree attainment and Algebra 1 achievement.

This variable remains statistically significant in the OLS model and becomes stronger at higher quantiles, indicating a consistent association across the achievement distribution.

This supports Sirin (2005),and Reardon et al. (2019), who emphasize parental education as a key driver of student success.

Answer to Research Question 2: Bachelor’s degree attainment is one of the strongest and most consistent predictors of Algebra 1 achievement.

Unemployment (Figure 5)

View R Code 
ggplot(final_data2, aes(x = unemployment_rate, y = Mean_Score)) +
geom_point(size = 3) +
geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
labs(
title = "Unemployment and Algebra 1 Achievement",
x = "Unemployment Rate",
y = "Mean Scale Score"
) +
theme_minimal()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Figure 5 shows a clear negative relationship between unemployment and Algebra 1 achievement.

Districts with higher unemployment rates consistently show lower achievement levels. Unlike poverty and income, unemployment remains statistically significant in the OLS model.

This finding aligns with Duncan and Murnane (2016), who show that economic instability directly affects student learning environments.

Answer to Research Question 2: Unemployment is a strong and consistent negative predictor of Algebra 1 achievement.

OLS Regression Results

View R Code 
ols_model2 <- lm(
Mean_Score ~
pct_white +
pct_black +
pct_hispanic +
poverty_rate +
unemployment_rate +
median_income +
bachelors_rate,
data = final_data2
)

summary(ols_model2)

Call:
lm(formula = Mean_Score ~ pct_white + pct_black + pct_hispanic + 
    poverty_rate + unemployment_rate + median_income + bachelors_rate, 
    data = final_data2)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-9.231 -2.880 -0.526  2.925  9.990 

Coefficients:
                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)        4.099e+02  3.124e+01  13.120   <2e-16 ***
pct_white         -1.943e-01  3.166e-01  -0.614   0.5419    
pct_black         -3.601e-01  3.318e-01  -1.085   0.2823    
pct_hispanic      -2.475e-01  3.123e-01  -0.793   0.4313    
poverty_rate       5.571e-02  2.427e-01   0.230   0.8193    
unemployment_rate -1.259e+00  5.278e-01  -2.386   0.0203 *  
median_income      1.355e-04  9.654e-05   1.403   0.1659    
bachelors_rate     3.781e-01  1.815e-01   2.084   0.0416 *  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 4.439 on 58 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6105,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.5635 
F-statistic: 12.99 on 7 and 58 DF,  p-value: 6.464e-10

The OLS regression shows that only unemployment and bachelor’s degree attainment are statistically significant predictors of Algebra 1 achievement.

Poverty, income, and demographic variables lose significance once all variables are included, suggesting overlapping explanatory power among SES indicators.

This reflects a limitation of mean-based regression, which assumes uniform effects across all districts. Koenker and Bassett (1978), highlight that this assumption may obscure important variation across the distribution.

Answer to Research Question 3: OLS identifies average relationships but does not capture variation across achievement levels.

Quantile Regression Analysis

Quantile regression was used to examine whether the relationships between demographic and socioeconomic variables and Algebra 1 achievement differ across the achievement distribution. Models were estimated at \(\tau = .10\), \(.25\), \(.50\), \(.75\), and \(.90\) to capture low-, middle-, and high-performing districts.

This approach directly addresses heterogeneity that is not observable in the OLS model by estimating effects at different points of the outcome distribution.

View R Code 
final_data2 <- final_data2 %>%
mutate(
Mean_Score = as.numeric(Mean_Score),
Level3Plus = as.numeric(Level3Plus)
)
View R Code 
library(quantreg)
Loading required package: SparseM
View R Code 
rq10 <- rq(
Mean_Score ~
pct_white +
pct_black +
pct_hispanic +
poverty_rate +
unemployment_rate +
median_income +
bachelors_rate,
tau = .10,
data = final_data2
)

rq25 <- rq(
Mean_Score ~
pct_white +
pct_black +
pct_hispanic +
poverty_rate +
unemployment_rate +
median_income +
bachelors_rate,
tau = .25,
data = final_data2
)

rq50 <- rq(
Mean_Score ~
pct_white +
pct_black +
pct_hispanic +
poverty_rate +
unemployment_rate +
median_income +
bachelors_rate,
tau = .50,
data = final_data2
)

rq75 <- rq(
Mean_Score ~
pct_white +
pct_black +
pct_hispanic +
poverty_rate +
unemployment_rate +
median_income +
bachelors_rate,
tau = .75,
data = final_data2
)

rq90 <- rq(
Mean_Score ~
pct_white +
pct_black +
pct_hispanic +
poverty_rate +
unemployment_rate +
median_income +
bachelors_rate,
tau = .90,
data = final_data2
)

summary(rq10)

Lowest-Achieving Districts (\(\tau = .10\))

At the 10th percentile, demographic variables show their strongest negative effects, including:

  • pct_white = \(-0.83163\)
  • pct_black = \(-0.88331\)
  • pct_hispanic = \(-0.81958\)

Unemployment is also strongly negative at this level.

These results suggest that structural disadvantage is most concentrated among the lowest-performing districts, where demographic composition and economic instability jointly reflect broader inequality.

This aligns with Owens (2017), who argue that racial disparities in achievement are driven primarily by structural and environmental inequality rather than individual characteristics.

Answer to Research Question 3: Predictor effects are strongest and most negative in low-achieving districts, particularly for demographic composition and unemployment.

Answer to Research Question 4: Race-related variables and unemployment are the strongest predictors at the lowest quantile.

Middle-Achieving Districts (\(\tau = .25\) and \(\tau = .50\))

At the 25th and 50th percentiles, demographic effects weaken substantially.

At the same time, bachelor’s degree attainment becomes increasingly important, while unemployment remains consistently negative.

This shift suggests that as districts move toward average performance, socioeconomic resources—particularly educational attainment—become more influential than demographic composition.

This pattern supports Reardon et al. (2019), who argues that educational attainment becomes increasingly important as students progress through higher achievement levels.

Answer to Research Question 3: Predictor effects change across the distribution, with demographic effects weakening and socioeconomic effects strengthening in middle-performing districts.

Answer to Research Question 4: Bachelor’s degree attainment becomes increasingly important in middle quantiles, while unemployment remains consistently negative. ### Highest-Achieving Districts (\(\tau = .75\) and \(\tau = .90\))

At higher quantiles, bachelor’s degree attainment becomes the dominant predictor, reaching \(0.47533\) at \(\tau = .90\).

Demographic variables become statistically insignificant, while unemployment remains negative and consistent.

This indicates that high-performing districts are primarily differentiated by levels of educational capital rather than demographic composition.

Bryk (2015) describe this pattern as the accumulation of advantage, where existing educational resources reinforce continued high performance.

Answer to Research Question 3: At higher achievement levels, socioeconomic effects—especially educational attainment—become dominant, while demographic effects diminish.

Answer to Research Question 4: Bachelor’s degree attainment is the strongest predictor at high quantiles, while unemployment remains consistently negative.

Correlation Matrix

View R Code 
library(dplyr)
library(corrplot)
corrplot 0.95 loaded
View R Code 
corr_data <- final_data2 %>%
select(
Mean_Score,
pct_white,
pct_black,
pct_hispanic,
poverty_rate,
unemployment_rate,
median_income,
bachelors_rate
)

cor_matrix <- cor(corr_data, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

corrplot(
cor_matrix,
method = "color",
type = "upper",
tl.cex = 0.9,
tl.col = "black",
number.cex = 0.7,
addCoef.col = "black",
col = colorRampPalette(c("#2166AC", "white", "#B2182B"))(200),
mar = c(0,0,1,0)
)

The correlation matrix shows:

  • Strong positive relationships between achievement, income (r = 0.72), and bachelor’s degree attainment (r = 0.65)
  • Strong negative relationship between poverty and income (r = –0.82)
  • Strong positive correlation between income and education (r = 0.82)

These patterns indicate strong multicollinearity among socioeconomic variables, explaining why some variables lose significance in regression models.

This supports Sirin (2005) and Reardon et al. (2019), who describe SES variables as a highly interconnected system rather than independent predictors.

Answer to Research Questions 1 & 2: District achievement is shaped by interrelated socioeconomic conditions rather than isolated variables.

Quantile Coefficient Plot

View R Code 
taus <- c(.10, .25, .50, .75, .90)

coef_data <- data.frame(
Quantile = taus,

pct_white = c(
coef(rq10)["pct_white"],
coef(rq25)["pct_white"],
coef(rq50)["pct_white"],
coef(rq75)["pct_white"],
coef(rq90)["pct_white"]
),

pct_black = c(
coef(rq10)["pct_black"],
coef(rq25)["pct_black"],
coef(rq50)["pct_black"],
coef(rq75)["pct_black"],
coef(rq90)["pct_black"]
),

pct_hispanic = c(
coef(rq10)["pct_hispanic"],
coef(rq25)["pct_hispanic"],
coef(rq50)["pct_hispanic"],
coef(rq75)["pct_hispanic"],
coef(rq90)["pct_hispanic"]
),

poverty_rate = c(
coef(rq10)["poverty_rate"],
coef(rq25)["poverty_rate"],
coef(rq50)["poverty_rate"],
coef(rq75)["poverty_rate"],
coef(rq90)["poverty_rate"]
),

unemployment_rate = c(
coef(rq10)["unemployment_rate"],
coef(rq25)["unemployment_rate"],
coef(rq50)["unemployment_rate"],
coef(rq75)["unemployment_rate"],
coef(rq90)["unemployment_rate"]
),

median_income = c(
coef(rq10)["median_income"],
coef(rq25)["median_income"],
coef(rq50)["median_income"],
coef(rq75)["median_income"],
coef(rq90)["median_income"]
),

bachelors_rate = c(
coef(rq10)["bachelors_rate"],
coef(rq25)["bachelors_rate"],
coef(rq50)["bachelors_rate"],
coef(rq75)["bachelors_rate"],
coef(rq90)["bachelors_rate"]
)
)

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

coef_long <- coef_data %>%
pivot_longer(
cols = -Quantile,
names_to = "Variable",
values_to = "Coefficient"
)

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(
coef_long,
aes(
x = Quantile,
y = Coefficient,
color = Variable
)
) +
geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
geom_point(size = 3) +
labs(
title = "Quantile Regression Coefficients Across Achievement Levels",
x = "Quantile",
y = "Coefficient Estimate"
) +
theme_minimal()

The coefficient plot confirms three key patterns:

  1. Unemployment is consistently negative across all quantiles
  2. Bachelor’s degree attainment increases steadily from low to high quantiles
  3. Demographic variables are strongest at low quantiles and weaken at higher quantiles

This clearly demonstrates heterogeneous effects that are not visible in OLS regression.

Koenker and Bassett (1978), highlight that quantile regression is specifically designed to uncover such distributional differences, which are critical in educational inequality research.

Answer to Research Question 3: There is clear evidence of heterogeneity in predictor effects across the achievement distribution.

Summary of Quantile Regression Findings

Overall, the results show:

  • Demographic variables matter most in low-achieving districts
  • Educational attainment matters most in high-achieving districts
  • Unemployment is consistently important across all levels

This confirms that educational inequality is not uniform but varies systematically across the achievement distribution.

Policy Implications

The findings of this study have important implications for educational policy in Florida. The results demonstrate that the determinants of Algebra 1 achievement vary across the achievement distribution, meaning that a single statewide intervention approach is unlikely to address all forms of educational inequality.

Target Economic Instability

Unemployment is one of the most consistent predictors of Algebra 1 achievement, remaining negative across all quantiles and statistically significant in the OLS model (\(p = 0.0203\)).

This suggests that economic instability affects student learning regardless of district performance level. Districts experiencing high unemployment should therefore receive targeted support, including tutoring programs, attendance interventions, and mental health services.

This aligns with Duncan and Murnane (2016), who show that economic instability disrupts learning environments and reduces academic performance.

Strengthen Educational Capital in Communities

Bachelor’s degree attainment is the strongest positive predictor, especially in higher-achieving districts (\(0.47533\) at \(\tau = .90\)).

This indicates that community educational attainment plays a key role in sustaining academic success.

Policies should therefore support adult education, workforce training, community college access, and family learning initiatives.

This is consistent with Sirin (2005) and Reardon et al. (2019), who emphasize the importance of parental education in shaping long-term educational outcomes.

Focus Intensive Support on Low-Achieving Districts

At the 10th percentile, demographic variables and unemployment show the strongest negative effects.

This indicates concentrated structural disadvantage in low-performing districts.

These districts require more intensive interventions such as:

  • Smaller class sizes
  • High-dosage tutoring
  • Extended learning time
  • Wraparound student services

This supports Fryer et al. (2015), who find that targeted interventions are most effective in high-poverty settings.

Avoid One-Size-Fits-All Policies

The results clearly show that predictor effects differ across achievement levels:

  • Demographic effects are strongest in low-performing districts
  • Educational attainment dominates in high-performing districts
  • Unemployment is consistently important

This means that uniform policies may fail to address local needs.

Bryk (2015) argue that effective school improvement must be context-specific, which is strongly supported by these findings.

Cross-Sector Policy Integration

The strong correlations among poverty, income, and education indicate that achievement is shaped by broader community systems.

Improving outcomes therefore requires coordination between:

  • Education systems
  • Workforce development
  • Housing policy
  • Public health services

This supports Reardon et al. (2019) and Owens (2017), who emphasize that achievement gaps are rooted in structural inequality beyond schools.

Contribution of the Study

This study makes several key contributions.

First, it applies quantile regression to district-level Algebra 1 achievement in Florida, revealing that predictor effects vary across the achievement distribution.

Second, it shows that socioeconomic variables—especially unemployment and educational attainment—are more consistent predictors of achievement than demographic composition alone.

Third, it demonstrates that educational inequality is not uniform. Instead, different mechanisms operate in low-, middle-, and high-achieving districts.

Finally, it highlights the importance of moving beyond OLS regression in educational inequality research, as average effects mask important distributional differences Koenker and Bassett (1978),.

Limitations

This study has several limitations.

First, the data are cross-sectional, meaning causal relationships cannot be established.

Second, the analysis is conducted at the district level, which limits interpretation at the individual student level and introduces potential ecological fallacy concerns.

Third, the study is limited to available socioeconomic and demographic variables. Important school-level factors such as teacher quality, attendance, and funding were not included.

Finally, the findings are specific to Florida and may not generalize to other states with different demographic and policy contexts.

Recommendations for Future Research

Future research should extend this work in several directions.

Longitudinal studies should be conducted to examine how these relationships change over time.

Multilevel models should be used to separate student-level and district-level effects.

Future studies should incorporate school-level variables such as attendance, instructional quality, and per-pupil funding.

Comparative studies across multiple states would also help determine whether these patterns are unique to Florida or nationally consistent.

Conclusion

This study examined the relationship between demographic and socioeconomic characteristics and Algebra 1 End-of-Course achievement across Florida school districts using both OLS and quantile regression.

The findings show that socioeconomic factors—especially unemployment and educational attainment—are the most consistent predictors of achievement.

Unemployment is a persistent negative predictor across all models and quantiles, while bachelor’s degree attainment becomes increasingly important at higher achievement levels.

Although poverty and income show strong bivariate relationships with achievement, their effects are largely explained by other socioeconomic variables.

The quantile regression results demonstrate that educational inequality is not uniform. Instead, predictor effects vary across the achievement distribution, with demographic variables being most important in low-performing districts and educational attainment being most important in high-performing districts.

Overall, the study highlights the importance of using distribution-sensitive methods such as quantile regression to better understand educational inequality and to design more targeted and effective policy interventions.

References

Bryk, Anthony S. 2015. “2014 AERA Distinguished Lecture: Accelerating How We Learn to Improve.” Educational Researcher 44 (9): 467–77. https://doi.org/10.3102/0013189X15621543.
Costanzo, Antonella, and Marta Desimoni. 2017. “Beyond the Mean Estimate: A Quantile Regression Analysis of Inequalities in Educational Outcomes Using INVALSI Survey Data.” Large-Scale Assessments in Education 5 (1): 14. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40536-017-0048-4.
Davino, Cristina, Marilena Furno, and Domenico Vistocco. 2013. Quantile Regression: Theory and Applications. John Wiley & Sons.
Duncan, Greg J., and Richard J. Murnane. 2016. “Rising Inequality in Family Incomes and Children’s Educational Outcomes.” RSF: The Russell Sage Foundation Journal of the Social Sciences 2 (2): 142–58. https://doi.org/10.7758/RSF.2016.2.2.06.
Fryer, Roland G., Jr., Steven D. Levitt, and John A. List. 2015. Parental Incentives and Early Childhood Achievement: A Field Experiment in Chicago Heights. Working Paper No. 21477. National Bureau of Economic Research. https://doi.org/10.3386/w21477.
Haile, Getinet Astatike, and Anh Ngoc Nguyen. 2008. “Determinants of Academic Attainment in the United States: A Quantile Regression Analysis of Test Scores.” Education Economics 16 (1): 29–57. https://doi.org/10.1080/09645290701523218.
Holley, Hope D. 2017. “A Quantitative Study Analyzing Predictive Factors That Affect Achievement on Florida’s Algebra i End-of-Course Exam (EOC).” Doctoral dissertation, Florida Southern College.
Koenker, Roger, and Gilbert Bassett Jr. 1978. “Regression Quantiles.” Econometrica 46 (1): 33–50. https://doi.org/10.2307/1913643.
Koenker, Roger, and Pin Ng. 2005. “Inequality Constrained Quantile Regression.” Sankhyā: The Indian Journal of Statistics 67 (2): 418–40.
Owens, Ann. 2017. “Income Segregation Between School Districts and Inequality in Students’ Achievement.” Sociology of Education 91 (1): 1–27. https://doi.org/10.1177/0038040717741180.
Reardon, Sean F., Demetra Kalogrides, and Kenneth Shores. 2019. “The Geography of Racial/Ethnic Test Score Gaps.” American Journal of Sociology 124 (4): 1164–221. https://doi.org/10.1086/700678.
Reeves, Edward B., and Jesse Lowe. 2009. “Quantile Regression: An Education Policy Research Tool.” Journal of Rural Social Sciences 24 (1): Article 10. https://egrove.olemiss.edu/jrss/vol24/iss1/10.
Sirin, Selcuk R. 2005. “Socioeconomic Status and Academic Achievement: A Meta-Analytic Review of Research.” Review of Educational Research 75 (3): 417–53. https://doi.org/10.3102/00346543075003417.
Tian, Maozai. 2006. “A Quantile Regression Analysis of Family Background Factor Effects on Mathematical Achievement.” Journal of Data Science 4 (4): 461–78.
